narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is above the typical student's 3.21—and your LSAT score of 39, which also exceeds the average of 36.70. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admission model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 52.4%, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school: your Year 3 decile of 1 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure, as it's well below the average of 5.53 and signals lower relative performance among peers. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also far under the typical 5.71, reinforced this concern as the next strongest influence in that direction. On a brighter note, your strong LSAT score worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive factor. Your above-average undergraduate GPA provided another meaningful boost toward passing, while your middle-income background and full-time status offered smaller but helpful nudges in the right direction. Compared to typical students, your early academic deciles stand out as unusually low, overshadowing your later achievements and putting you at higher predicted risk than most applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes bar passage patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring for undergrad-level skills, and reapplying after strengthening those early indicators; schools value persistence, and your LSAT and GPA give you a strong foundation to build on. We're rooting for you to turn this into your success story.
